united-states-colored-troops.caption: Company E, 4th U.S. Colored Infantry, at Fort Lincoln in the defenses of Washington, c.1864.
united-states-colored-troops.alt: A full company of Black Union soldiers of the 4th U.S. Colored Infantry standing at attention in two ranks before a fort, c.1864.
united-states-colored-troops.periodNote: A genuine 1864 photograph of a USCT company in the field — period-accurate to the war.
united-states-colored-troops.file: File:District of Columbia. Company E, 4th U.S. Colored Infantry, at Fort Lincoln LOC cwpb.04294.jpg
united-states-colored-troops.artist: Smith, William Morris; Civil War Glass Negatives
united-states-colored-troops.credit: Library of Congress Catalog: https://www.loc.gov/pictures/item/cwp2003000946/PP Original url: http://hdl.loc.gov/loc.pnp/cwpb.04294

54th-massachusetts-infantry.caption: Sgt. William H. Carney, 54th Massachusetts — the first Black soldier whose deeds earned the Medal of Honor, holding the regimental colors he saved at Fort Wagner.
54th-massachusetts-infantry.alt: Sergeant William H. Carney in uniform holding the United States flag he carried at the assault on Fort Wagner.
54th-massachusetts-infantry.periodNote: A wartime portrait of the 54th Massachusetts color-sergeant — period-accurate.
54th-massachusetts-infantry.credit: Scanned from the book African American Recipients of the Medal of Honor by Charles W. Hanna (2002, McFarland & Company, Inc.)

robert-gould-shaw.caption: Col. Robert Gould Shaw, the abolitionist-born commander of the 54th Massachusetts, killed leading its assault on Battery Wagner, July 18, 1863.
robert-gould-shaw.alt: A seated portrait photograph of the young Union colonel Robert Gould Shaw in his uniform and kepi.
robert-gould-shaw.periodNote: A wartime portrait of the 54th Massachusetts colonel — period-accurate.

frederick-douglass.caption: Frederick Douglass in the 1860s — the formerly enslaved abolitionist whose "Men of Color, To Arms!" helped raise the USCT.
frederick-douglass.alt: A formal portrait photograph of Frederick Douglass in the 1860s, seated in a suit.
frederick-douglass.periodNote: An 1860s portrait of the wartime abolitionist — period-accurate.
frederick-douglass.artist: Merrill & Crosby imprint on the reverse.

harriet-tubman.caption: Harriet Tubman, who guided the 1863 Combahee River Raid that freed some 750 enslaved people — the first woman to lead a U.S. military operation.
harriet-tubman.alt: A carte-de-visite portrait photograph of Harriet Tubman seated, one arm resting on a chair back.
harriet-tubman.periodNote: Benjamin F. Powelson carte-de-visite, Auburn NY, c.1868-69 — a postwar studio portrait, made a few years after Tubman's wartime service.
harriet-tubman.credit: Collection of the National Museum of African American History and Culture shared with the Library of Congress

nathan-bedford-forrest.caption: The Northern press depicts the massacre of surrendering U.S. Colored Troops at Fort Pillow, April 12, 1864 (Harper's Weekly, April 30, 1864).
nathan-bedford-forrest.alt: An 1864 Harper's Weekly engraving showing Confederate troops killing surrendering Black Union soldiers at Fort Pillow.
nathan-bedford-forrest.periodNote: A contemporary 1864 newspaper engraving condemning the atrocity — period source, anti-Lost-Cause.
nathan-bedford-forrest.file: File:The Massacre at Fort Pillow - unsigned - Harper's Weekly - issue of April 30, 1864 - page 284.jpg

system-manpower-pool-usct.caption: "Come and Join Us Brothers" — an 1863 broadside of the Supervisory Committee for Recruiting Colored Regiments.
system-manpower-pool-usct.alt: An 1863 recruitment broadside showing Black Union soldiers in formation beside the flag, captioned "Come and Join Us Brothers".
system-manpower-pool-usct.file: File:Come and Join Us Brothers, by the Supervisory Committee For Recruiting Colored Regiments.jpg
system-manpower-pool-usct.artist: The Supervisory Committee For Recruiting Colored Regiments
